title: Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1 (Reading & Use of English)

sections.title: Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read three extracts which are all concerned in some way with human behaviour. For questions 1-6, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
Extract 1: Extract from a Book Review

Why do vast flocks of birds, driven as much by some irrepressible genetic impulse as by the dictates of seasonal change, migrate thousands of miles every year? And why do people, torn between 'the known and the new', so often oscillate between the prospect of the journey and the draw of home? Under the pretext of exploring the first question, The Snow Geese, William Fiennes's quirky and autobiographical first book, takes us to the heart of the second.

The story begins with Fiennes confined to a hospital bed, stricken by a curious and psychologically debilitating illness, dreaming of the comforts and protection of his family home. But when he returns to the familiarity of the old ironstone house, restlessness soon replaces homesickness. He longs to be jolted out of his introspection. Paul Gallico's novel The Snow Goose becomes an unlikely inspiration, and Fiennes resolves to follow the migratory path of real-life snow geese as they fly from Texas to the Canadian Arctic Circle on their annual spring voyage. It is emotional healing through the power of bird-watching, allowing him to put the past behind him. The result is an original blend of travel writing, autobiography and reportage.

Extract 2: Tools

Anthropologists often say that what distinguishes humans from other primates is the ability to use tools. Unfortunately, they don't tell us how, while using those tools, the human in question - in this case yours truly - is supposed not to mislay them continually. As a qualified bricklayer, I should say at the outset that I never have a problem with my bricklaying tools. It was drummed into me that, when not in my hand, my trowel would be stuck in the pile of mortar on the spot board, my tape would be in my left-hand pocket, my spirit level would be leaning against the brick stack and my pencil behind my right ear. If I have even the slightest temptation to put these items anywhere else, then my instructor's voice jumps out from the recesses of my memory and gives me a loud ticking-off. So these tools are always to hand.

But, with other jobs, I've never been able to get into the same kind of habit. Screwdrivers, pincers, spanners - one minute I'm using them, the next they've disappeared. I can spend more time looking for a screwdriver than actually using it. Really tidy people hang their tools on hooks fixed to a pegboard on the wall of their workshop, and draw the outline of each tool with a felt-tipped pen, so they can see where everything belongs and spot if something is missing. I admire these people immensely, but something in my character prevents me ever doing this myself. I did once get as far as buying a sheet of pegboard, but then I mislaid it.

Extract 3: Book Crossing

My heart was thumping. Surreptitiously, I scanned the carriage. The train coasted into the platform, and the briefcase-clutching, umbrella-wielding crowd made its way towards the door. I slid my book - Hanif Kureishi's Love in a Blue Time - between two seats. A quick glance at my fellow passengers: no one saw. Off the train, and up the escalator - I increased my pace and pulled down my hat. Almost out of the station. I had done it! I had 'released' my first book. As my smile spread, I noticed rapidly approaching footsteps from behind. A tap on my shoulder: 'Excuse me, Miss,' said a kindly man, 'I think you left this behind.' Bother!

The Book Crossing organisation - nay, movement - claims, with 250,000 members globally, to be the largest book group in the world. Here's how it works. You register with the site (www.bookcrossing.com), tag one of your books with a special Book Crossing number generated by the website, and then 'release it to the wild'. With any luck, your book will then be rescued - or, as they say, captured. The book-catcher is invited, via use of the Book Crossing code number and website, to update the travels of the book, read and exchange their impressions of it and then, in true Book Crossing spirit, pass it on.

questions.instruction: 1. In the first paragraph, the reviewer suggests that Fiennes' book
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 1, Trang 7. Chọn A. Review mở bằng hai câu hỏi song song: chim di cư và con người bị kéo giữa 'the known and the new'. Câu chốt 'takes us to the heart of the second' cho thấy trọng tâm thật là cảm giác giằng co của con người trước việc rời đi hay trở về. Trap: B bám keyword 'birds/migrate' nhưng bài chim chỉ là cái cớ để nói về hành vi con người.
questions.answerLocation: Under the pretext of exploring bird migration, the book 'takes us to the heart' of why people oscillate between journey and home.
options: A. seeks to understand people's mixed feelings about travel.
B. throws new light on the migratory habits of birds.
C. rejects comparisons between human and bird behaviour.
D. fails to answer satisfactorily either of the questions it poses.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 2. What do we learn about Fiennes in the second paragraph?
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 1, Trang 7. Chọn C. Sau khi trở về nhà, 'restlessness soon replaces homesickness' và Fiennes 'longs to be jolted out of his introspection', rồi quyết định theo dấu đàn ngỗng tuyết như một cách hồi phục tinh thần. Trap: B có vẻ gần vì có Paul Gallico's novel, nhưng đề nói 'book he read in hospital' trong khi passage không nói ông đọc cuốn đó ở bệnh viện.
questions.answerLocation: He longs to be jolted out of introspection and follows the snow geese as emotional healing.
options: A. His state of health prevents him from travelling.
B. He has been inspired by a book he read in hospital.
C. He is looking for something to occupy his mind positively.
D. He has written his book as a way of overcoming his illness.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 3. The writer attributes his tidiness with his bricklaying tools to
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 1, Trang 8. Chọn D. Người viết nói thói quen đặt trowel, tape, spirit level, pencil đúng chỗ đã được 'drummed into me', và chỉ cần định đặt sai là giọng thầy hướng dẫn lại vang lên trong trí nhớ. Trap: C nghe hợp lý vì ông là bricklayer, nhưng passage nhấn vào huấn luyện cụ thể chứ không phải kinh nghiệm lâu năm.
questions.answerLocation: It was 'drummed into me' where each tool should be; the instructor's voice still corrects him.
options: A. his need to use them on a regular basis.
B. the fact that he has a good memory.
C. his years of experience in the trade.
D. the effectiveness of his training.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 4. In the piece as a whole, the writer is
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 1, Trang 8. Chọn B. Giọng văn tự trào: ngoài dụng cụ xây, ông liên tục làm mất screwdriver/spanner và thậm chí mua pegboard rồi cũng làm mất. Trap: A chỉ là câu mở gây cười về anthropologists, không phải ý chính của toàn đoạn.
questions.answerLocation: I did once get as far as buying a sheet of pegboard, but then I mislaid it.
options: A. casting doubt on an academic theory.
C. making fun of people who are too tidy.
D. explaining how tools should be maintained.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 5. In the first paragraph, the writer describes a situation in which she
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 1, Trang 9. Chọn A. Cô ấy nghĩ mình đã hoàn thành việc 'release' cuốn sách ('I had done it!'), nhưng niềm vui chỉ kéo dài vài giây trước khi người đàn ông trả lại sách. Trap: B sai vì người đàn ông không phát hiện ý định Book Crossing; ông chỉ tưởng cô bỏ quên sách.
questions.answerLocation: 'I had done it!' is immediately followed by a man returning the book: 'I think you left this behind.'
options: A. felt short-lived satisfaction in an achievement.
B. was embarrassed when her intentions were uncovered.
C. became resigned to her inability to do something successfully.
D. had feelings of frustration towards someone who intended to spoil her plans.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 6. The organisation described in the second paragraph aims to
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 1, Trang 9. Chọn C. Book Crossing yêu cầu người bắt được sách cập nhật hành trình, đọc, trao đổi cảm nhận rồi chuyền tiếp. Trọng tâm là chia sẻ và thảo luận quanh sách đã xuất bản, không phải mua sách hay đọc online. Trap: B bị kéo bởi website, nhưng website chỉ dùng để theo dõi sách vật lý.
questions.answerLocation: The book-catcher is invited to update the travels, read and exchange impressions, then pass the book on.
options: A. promote the work of certain authors.
B. encourage people to read online novels.
C. provoke discussion of certain published works.
D. persuade people to buy more books.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

sections.title: Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an extract from a magazine article. Six paragraphs have been removed from the extract. Choose from the paragraphs A-G the one which fits each gap (7-12). There is one extra paragraph which you do not need to use.
sections.passageHtml:
The Magic Lute

Four hundred years ago, the royal courts of Europe resounded to strains of the lute. Then the instrument did a mysterious vanishing act. Arthur Robb is one of a small band of craftsmen bringing the instrument back from the past.

Arthur Robb has been marching to a different tune all his life. When the youth of Europe was listening to the Beatles and the Rolling Stones, he went to Paris and Amsterdam as part of a classical choir. And then in swinging London, he discovered even earlier music. It has all been good training, though. Now in his fifties, he is recognised as a leading expert in one of contemporary music's most fashionable offshoots - the revival of interest in the ancient string instrument, the lute.

Gap 7

Yet lutes were once produced in astonishing numbers. When the celebrated Italian lute maker Laux Maler died in 1552, an inventory of his workshop revealed more than a thousand lutes in various stages of construction. The instrument's disappearance was so dramatic, however, that very few early examples survive.

Gap 8

What happened to all the others is a mystery. Robb's theory is that the lute was killed off by the development of keyboard instruments like the pianoforte. But the end must have come suddenly. Some of the last music for solo lute was written by J. S. Bach. Within years of his death in 1750, the instrument which had dominated Europe's musical repertoire for centuries had all but vanished.

Gap 9

Digging into literature and old manuscripts, such as early musical scores, has allowed him to discover how the music might have sounded, whilst the examination of old paintings gives clues as to the details of the instrument's design. The lute has certainly altered over time, evolving from an elongated oval to a deep pear-shape. The stringing and the sound produced must also have changed as a result. 'The lute is like a time machine,' says Robb. 'Its history goes back into antiquity, possibly to ancient Egypt.'

Gap 10

Lute music is considered rather quiet compared with the volume of today's orchestration. But centuries ago, when music was being written for the instrument, people's ears were better attuned to quieter sounds.

Gap 11

Despite his enthusiasm, his initial efforts did not meet with immediate approval. A novice carpenter, he practised for a year, making wooden toys and household items to improve his basic skills, before joining an adult education class in musical instrument making. After months of meticulous work, he proudly offered a completed lute to a music shop in Bristol.

Gap 12

Far from being discouraged, Robb set about putting things to rights. Modern-day lute makers have problems their craftsmen forebears could never have imagined. Worldwide concern about the use of rare timber, for example, has meant that he has had to adapt his methods to the materials that are most readily available. He has, however, gone on to make dozens of lutes, each finer than the last, and repaired many more.

Paragraph options (A-G):

A. Those that do are now priceless museum pieces, and even these treasured relics have been damaged or altered so much during their life that copying them doesn't guarantee historical accuracy.

B. What's more, no authentic plan of a genuine fifteenth- or sixteenth-century lute has ever been found, and so no one knows what tools were used to make the instruments. Robb, alongside fellow enthusiasts in Britain and the USA, has been spearheading the lute's revival. This means unearthing fragments of information from surrounding strata like archaeologists hunting a fossil.

C. In turning it down, they left him in no doubt as to the shortcomings of his creation. It was the wrong shape, the wrong weight, the strings were too long to achieve the right pitch and the pegs which tightened the strings were too bulky for comfort.

D. But so little factual evidence remains, even from more recent times, that Robb has to think himself back in time in order to begin to see how they should be made. Only by appreciating the way people lived, how they behaved and the technology they used, can he begin to piece together the complete picture.

E. 'Appreciating small nuances like that is vital to an appreciation of how the instrument might have been played,' Robb says. As one of a small band of professional lute makers who keep in touch via the Internet, Robb can share these impressions, as well as swapping problems and possible solutions. No such forum existed when Robb began to construct his first lute 25 years ago, however. He had to work things out on his own.

F. Robb's enquiries have, however, punctured one other popular myth - that of the lute player as a wandering minstrel. Almost from its introduction into Europe, the lute was a wealthy person's instrument, the players attaining a status comparable to modern-day concert pianists.

G. From a tiny attic workshop in the English countryside, Robb makes exquisite examples of this forgotten instrument. Piecing together the few remaining clues to the instrument's construction and musical characteristics has demanded all his single-minded concentration.

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct paragraph from A–G to fill each gap.

questions.instruction: 7. Gap 7
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 2, Trang 10-11. Chọn G. Gap 7 follows the statement that Robb is now a leading expert; option G identifies his attic workshop and his work making lutes. It also introduces the scarcity of clues, which sets up the next sentence: “Yet lutes were once produced in astonishing numbers.” Method: đọc câu trước/sau gap, tìm đại từ tham chiếu và logic thời gian. Trap: không chọn option chỉ vì có cùng keyword “lute”; option phải nối được cả hai phía của gap.
questions.answerLocation: Option G introduces Robb making lutes from the remaining clues.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.instruction: 8. Gap 8
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 2, Trang 10-11. Chọn A. Before Gap 8, the passage says very few early lutes survive. Option A refers back to “those that do” survive and explains why even surviving museum pieces cannot simply be copied. The next sentence then asks what happened to all the others. Method: đọc câu trước/sau gap, tìm đại từ tham chiếu và logic thời gian. Trap: không chọn option chỉ vì có cùng keyword “lute”; option phải nối được cả hai phía của gap.
questions.answerLocation: Those that do are now priceless museum pieces
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.instruction: 9. Gap 9
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 2, Trang 10-11. Chọn B. After the sudden disappearance of the lute, the text needs to explain how Robb can reconstruct it. Option B says no authentic plan has been found and that Robb’s revival requires unearthing fragments of information, which leads directly into literature, manuscripts and paintings. Method: đọc câu trước/sau gap, tìm đại từ tham chiếu và logic thời gian. Trap: không chọn option chỉ vì có cùng keyword “lute”; option phải nối được cả hai phía của gap.
questions.answerLocation: no authentic plan of a genuine fifteenth- or sixteenth-century lute has ever been found
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 2, Trang 10-11. Chọn D. Option D says Robb must think himself back in time and understand how people lived. The following paragraph gives exactly that historical context: quieter music and ears better attuned to quieter sounds. Trap: F is about social status, not the immediate evidence-to-context link. Method: đọc câu trước/sau gap, tìm đại từ tham chiếu và logic thời gian. Trap: không chọn option chỉ vì có cùng keyword “lute”; option phải nối được cả hai phía của gap.
questions.answerLocation: Robb has to think himself back in time
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 2, Trang 10-11. Chọn E. The paragraph before Gap 11 says listeners centuries ago heard quiet lute music differently. Option E begins by calling such nuances vital, then explains that Robb had to work out early construction problems alone. This prepares for the next paragraph about his first failed lute. Method: đọc câu trước/sau gap, tìm đại từ tham chiếu và logic thời gian. Trap: không chọn option chỉ vì có cùng keyword “lute”; option phải nối được cả hai phía của gap.
questions.answerLocation: Appreciating small nuances like that is vital
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 1, Reading Part 2, Trang 10-11. Chọn C. Before Gap 12, Robb offers his first lute to a music shop. Option C explains that the shop turned it down and lists the technical faults. The next sentence, “Far from being discouraged,” only makes sense after that rejection. Method: đọc câu trước/sau gap, tìm đại từ tham chiếu và logic thời gian. Trap: không chọn option chỉ vì có cùng keyword “lute”; option phải nối được cả hai phía của gap.
questions.answerLocation: they left him in no doubt as to the shortcomings of his creation
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

sections.title: Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read a magazine article about the crime writer P.D. James. For questions 13–19, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
P.D. James: The Queen of Crime

P.D. James, the author of more than a dozen bestselling detective novels, is often described as the successor to Agatha Christie. It is a comparison she accepts with a mix of gratitude and mild irritation. 'Agatha Christie was a superb storyteller, and she did more than anyone to popularize the genre,' James says, sitting in the drawing room of her London home. 'But her novels are essentially puzzles. They are two-dimensional. I want to write novels that are three-dimensional, where the characters have pasts and futures, and where the setting is as important as the plot. I want to write a good novel that happens to be a detective story, not just a detective story that happens to be a novel.'

This ambition has sometimes led to accusations that James is trying to raise the status of crime fiction above its station, a charge she denies. 'I don't think there is any need to be defensive about crime fiction,' she says. 'It has a long and honourable history, from Wilkie Collins and Arthur Conan Doyle to Dorothy L. Sayers and Raymond Chandler. It is a form that can accommodate serious moral and social themes. But it is also a demanding form. A new crime writer often thinks that because the genre is popular, it must be easy to write. They soon discover that structuring a complex plot, keeping the reader guessing, and maintaining suspense while also developing realistic characters is a formidable challenge. It is much harder than it looks.'

For James, the key to a successful crime novel lies in the tension between order and chaos. 'A crime novel is essentially about the disruption of a social order by a violent act, and the subsequent efforts to restore that order,' she explains. 'In that sense, it is a very conservative form. But it is also a very satisfying one. In a world that is increasingly chaotic and unpredictable, where we are constantly bombarded with news of violence and injustice that is never resolved, the crime novel offers a comforting narrative arc. A crime is committed, it is investigated, and it is solved. Order is restored. That is why it is so popular. It provides reassurance.' [line 41]

This sense of order is reflected in James's own life and work. Her study is meticulously organized, with reference books arranged alphabetically and drafts of her current novel filed neatly in colour-coded folders. Her prose style is similarly ordered — precise, clear, and 'uncluttered' by unnecessary ornament. [line 61] 'I like things to be in their place,' she admits. 'I think that is a characteristic of many crime writers. We are, by nature, organizers. We want to make sense of the world, even if it is only the world of our own creation.'

Despite her reputation for meticulous planning, James says she still enjoys the element of discovery in writing. 'I know the beginning and I know the end, and I have a very clear idea of the characters,' she says. 'But the journey between the two is always full of surprises. Sometimes a character will do or say something that I hadn't planned, and I have to follow them down a new path. That is the joy of writing. If I knew exactly what was going to happen on every page, I would get bored, and if I am bored, the reader will be bored too.'

Now in her eighties, James shows no signs of slowing down. She still writes every morning, sitting at her desk by seven o’clock. 'I prefer the morning,' she says. 'The mind is fresher then, and the house is quiet. By afternoon, the world starts to intrude — phone calls, letters, visitors. But in the early morning, I can live entirely in my own world. It is the only place I really want to be.'

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter A, B, C or D.

questions.instruction: 13. What does the writer suggest about P.D. James's stance on crime fiction?
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 13 Chọn C — 'she is defensive of it...'. Trong đoạn 2, văn bản ghi: 'This ambition has sometimes led to accusations that James is trying to raise the status of crime fiction... a charge she denies. "I don't think there is any need to be defensive about crime fiction," she says. It has a long and honourable history...'. Điều này cho thấy bà bảo vệ thể loại này chống lại những cáo buộc hạ thấp giá trị của nó, khẳng định nó có lịch sử danh giá và khả năng chứa đựng các chủ đề đạo đức, xã hội nghiêm túc. ❌ A sai vì bà không nghĩ nó vượt trội hơn các thể loại khác mà chỉ bảo vệ giá trị vốn có của nó. ❌ B sai vì bà không muốn thay đổi các quy ước cơ bản mà chỉ muốn viết sâu sắc hơn. ❌ D sai vì bà không tỏ ra bối rối về sự thành công của nó. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. She is defensive of its literary merit and history. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. She believes it is superior to other literary genres.
B. She wishes to change its fundamental conventions.
C. She is defensive of its literary merit and history.
D. She is embarrassed by its popular appeal.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 14. James's comment on 'a new crime writer' is a warning that
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 13 Chọn A — 'writing it is harder than it looks.'. James chỉ ra: 'A new crime writer often thinks that because the genre is popular, it must be easy to write. They soon discover that structuring a complex plot... is a formidable challenge. It is much harder than it looks.' Đây là lời cảnh báo trực tiếp rằng việc viết tiểu thuyết trinh thám phức tạp và khó khăn hơn nhiều so với suy nghĩ đơn giản ban đầu của các cây viết mới. ❌ B sai vì không nói về việc bắt chước các nhà văn khác. ❌ C sai vì không đề cập đến việc tìm nhà xuất bản. ❌ D sai vì bà không khuyên họ tránh xa thể loại này. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. writing it is harder than it looks. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. writing it is harder than it looks.
B. they should not copy established authors.
C. finding a publisher can be very difficult.
D. they should focus on other genres first.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 15. In James's view, what makes a crime novel successful?
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 13 Chọn B — 'satisfying plot and structure...'. Trong văn bản, James nhấn mạnh tầm quan trọng của việc xây dựng một cốt truyện phức tạp, duy trì sự hồi hộp đồng thời phát triển các nhân vật thực tế ('structuring a complex plot, keeping the reader guessing, and maintaining suspense while also developing realistic characters'). ❌ A sai vì bà chê tiểu thuyết của Agatha Christie là chỉ như những câu đố hai chiều ('essentially puzzles... two-dimensional'). ❌ C sai vì các yếu tố bạo lực chỉ là điểm bắt đầu gây rối loạn trật tự chứ không phải chìa khóa thành công. ❌ D sai vì bà không nói sự độc đáo của thám tử là quan trọng nhất. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

sections.title: Part 4: Multiple Matching (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read the introduction to a book about organising local events. For questions 20–34, choose from the sections (A–E). The sections may be chosen more than once.
sections.passageHtml:
Organising Local Events

Sarah Passingham, a professional fundraiser, offers advice on how to organise successful local events.

Section A

I have worked with a good many groups over the years, and I have seen events run beautifully and others turn into disasters. When things go wrong, it is desperately disappointing and disheartening for everyone involved. Perhaps by looking at some of my suggestions and learning from my mistakes, most of those disappointing times can be averted. However, this guide is not meant as the definitive work on organising events; rather it is meant as a framework on which to hang your own ideas and methods of doing things. One major difference between my own position and that of most local organisers is that I was being paid for my many hours of toil, whereas the vast majority of local volunteers work for no financial remuneration whatsoever.

Section B

Organising an event is hard work, and there have been times when I have felt so overwhelmed that I came close to falling apart on occasions. Sometimes what the volunteers were experiencing was far removed from the original concept we had planned, but nobody minded and, more often than not, nobody knew. It is easy to get caught up in the details and lose sight of the big picture, but I hope this book will act as a buffer between you and the chaos, and that it will allow you not only to appear calm and well organised, but genuinely to be those things. The feeling of being in control of a major project, rather than being swept along by it, is incredibly satisfying.

Section C

Committees can be tiresome, unwieldy groups, and I have sat through meetings where some members seemed to enjoy nothing more than social chit-chat and had no real desire to do any actual work. However, if you have a committee that operates efficiently and decisively, it can relieve you of much of the time-consuming but necessary work, and allow you to get to grips with the real nitty-gritty of the event. It is always worth having at least a cashier or treasurer as a second person to keep an eye on your balance or banking. You never want to be put in the position of having the finger of doubt or suspicion pointed at you, and having someone else check the figures is a simple safeguard for everyone.

Section D

My natural instinct, I must admit, is to want to do everything myself, but an unwillingness to delegate is a fatal flaw in an event organiser. You need to identify the strengths of your volunteers and match them to the tasks. Some people care passionately about the cause and will work tirelessly, while others may simply want to offer their obvious muscle and brawn for heavy lifting on the day. Both are valuable, and a good organiser knows how to make use of both. If you try to do it all, you will not only burn out, but you will also alienate the very people who have offered to help you.

Section E

Volunteers need to know that their contribution is valued and that they are not just being taken for granted. Always show that you need your volunteers, and thank them publicly and privately. In my experience, as long as they are prepared beforehand, people will have a go at almost anything, but they do need clear guidelines and a sense of direction. Some people actually prefer allowing someone else to hold the ultimate responsibility, finding it a relief to simply follow instructions. By establishing a clear chain of command, you can ensure that everyone knows what they are doing and who they should go to if a problem arises.

sections.passageHtml:
Secretaries

What's in a name? In the case of the secretary, or Personal Assistant (PA), it can be something rather surprising. The dictionary calls a secretary 'anyone who (0) handles correspondence, keeps records and does clerical work for others'. But while this particular job (1) ..... looks a bit (2) ....., the word’s original meaning is a hundred times more exotic and perhaps more (3) ...... The word itself has been with us since the 14th century and comes from the mediaeval Latin word secretarius meaning 'something hidden'. Secretaries started out as those members of staff with knowledge hidden from others, the silent ones mysteriously (4) ..... the secret machinery of organisations.

Some years ago 'something hidden' probably meant (5) ..... out of sight, tucked away with all the other secretaries and typists. A good secretary was an unremarkable one, efficiently (6) ..... orders, and then returning mouse-like to his or her station behind the typewriter, but, with the (7) ..... of new office technology, the job (8) ..... upgraded itself and the role has changed to one closer to the original meaning. The skills required are more demanding and more technical. Companies are (9) ..... that secretarial staff should already be (10) ..... trained in, and accustomed to working with, a (11) ..... of word processing packages. Professionals in the (12) ..... business point out that nowadays secretarial staff may even need some management skills to take on administration, personnel work and research.

Example (0): A. deals | B. handles | C. runs | D. controls (Answer: B)

sections.passageHtml:
Changing cities

What will the city of (0) the future look like? This question has been asked (13) ..... many times in recent history — and answered inconclusively (14) ..... equal number of times — that we (15) ..... be sure of (16) ..... thing only: no one can predict with (17) ..... degree of accuracy how cities will look 50 or 500 years from now.

The reason is simple — cities are (18) ..... a continual state of change. Over the (19) ..... fifty years they have changed so rapidly that the oldest residents will remember a time (20) ..... their city seemed to belong not just to another era (21) ..... to a different dimension.

(22) ..... is true both of planned and unplanned cities. Planned cities such as New York and Paris, (23) ..... are closely organised on a grid or diagram of streets and avenues, have effectively burst at the seams this century, while unplanned cities such as Tokyo and Los Angeles have grown just (24) ..... dramatically. Although their centres might remain much as they were many years (25) ....., their suburbs have spread (26) ..... the tentacles of an octopus.

Some economists argue that expansion is a sign of a healthy economy (27) ..... that it is expanding cities that attract international investment.

Example (0): THE

Snow-kiting: an alternative form of skiing?

Skiing is one of the most (0) straightforward forms of exercise there is. It offers the participant, whether a novice or an old hand at the sport, a great deal of excitement and (28) ....., plus lots of fresh air. But skiing does have its various (29) ..... . When it comes to guaranteeing a profitable day’s downhill skiing, there are two essential ingredients. You need hills, or (30) ..... mountains, in order to get the most out of this (31) .....; then you need a fairly generous covering of snow. (32) ....., there are usually snow machines to supplement any natural (33) ..... in the supply of this second (34) ....., and help may now be at hand too for those lacking in the first, through the sport of snow-kiting.

Snow-kiting is a wintry offshoot of kite-surfing, an established watersport. By harnessing their skis to an inflated kite, snow-kiters can move at speed across even the very flattest of landscapes. All they need is a (35) ..... wind, then they can enjoy all the exhilaration of a fast downhill ski run. In fact, partly because of (36) ..... objects such as electricity pylons and trees, the best location for snow-kiting is not a ski resort at all — but a vast (37) ..... plain. Skiing may never be the same again.
